name: Real Numbers — Rational, Irrational and the HCF·LCM Link
whatIsIt: Real numbers are every number on the number line. Some are RATIONAL (can be written as a fraction p/q, like 3/4 or 0.5) and some are IRRATIONAL (never a neat fraction, never a repeating or ending decimal, like √2 or π). Two key Class-10 ideas: for any two numbers, HCF × LCM = the product of the numbers; and a fraction gives a TERMINATING decimal exactly when its lowest-term denominator is made of only 2s and 5s.
whyNeeded: Knowing whether a number is rational or irrational, and using HCF × LCM = product, lets you simplify surds, predict whether a fraction ends or repeats, and solve real problems like when two blinking lights line up again (LCM) or the largest equal group size (HCF).
realLifeUses: Deciding the largest tile that fits a floor exactly (HCF)
Finding when two bells or lights sync again (LCM)
Knowing whether a division gives a clean decimal or repeats forever
Simplifying square roots in measurement and construction

vocabulary.meaning: A number that can be written as p/q with whole numbers p and q (q ≠ 0); its decimal ends or repeats.
A number that is NOT a fraction; its decimal goes on forever with no repeat, like √2 = 1.41421… or π.
A decimal that ends. Happens when the fraction's lowest-term denominator has only factors 2 and 5.
For two numbers a and b: HCF(a, b) × LCM(a, b) = a × b.

story.title: The Two Blinking Lamps
story.text: On Diwali, one lamp blinks every 12 seconds and another every 18 seconds. 'When do they blink together?' Nisha asks. Dadu says: 'That's the LCM.' They also spot HCF(12, 18) = 6. Dadu smiles: 'Check the rule — HCF × LCM should equal 12 × 18.' Since 12 × 18 = 216 and HCF = 6, the LCM must be 216 ÷ 6 = 36 seconds. The lamps blink together every 36 seconds.
story.extractedProblem: HCF(12, 18) = 6 and 12 × 18 = 216. What is the LCM?
story.answerInStory: 36 — because HCF × LCM = product, so LCM = 216 ÷ 6 = 36.

props.label: √2 ≈ 1.41 and √5 ≈ 2.24 sit between whole numbers — irrational points on the line

visual.caption: Rational numbers (like 1, 2, 3) and irrational ones (like √2 ≈ 1.41) all live on the real number line.

teachingGallery.title: Ends or repeats?
teachingGallery.note: A fraction terminates only when its lowest-term denominator is built from 2s and 5s.

examples.caption: 3 is not 2 or 5 → 1/3 repeats forever, it does not end.

standardMethod.summary: Classify by whether a number is a fraction (rational) or not (irrational). Use HCF × LCM = product to find a missing HCF or LCM, and check the denominator's prime factors to predict terminating decimals.
steps: To find a missing HCF or LCM of two numbers, use HCF × LCM = a × b and divide.
To test if p/q terminates: reduce to lowest terms, factor the denominator; if only 2s and 5s appear, it terminates, else it repeats.
A number like √n is irrational unless n is a perfect square (√9 = 3 is rational).
Check: rational decimals end or repeat; irrational decimals never do.

workedExamples.problem: HCF(12, 18) = 6. Find the LCM using HCF × LCM = product.

steps: Only the prime 2 appears (no other primes).

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking every square root is irrational.
commonMistakes.fix: √ of a PERFECT SQUARE is rational: √9 = 3, √16 = 4. Only non-perfect-squares like √2, √5 are irrational.

commonMistakes.mistake: Judging if a fraction terminates before reducing it (e.g. 6/12 looks like it has a 3, but reduces to 1/2).
commonMistakes.fix: Reduce to lowest terms FIRST. 6/12 = 1/2, denominator 2 → it terminates (0.5).

easy.q: HCF × LCM = 200 and HCF = 10. What is the LCM?

medium.q: For 15 and 20, HCF = 5. Since 15 × 20 = 300, find the LCM.
Which fraction gives a TERMINATING decimal?
hintLadder: Terminates if denominator is only 2s and 5s.

challenge.q: Two numbers have HCF 6 and LCM 72. If one number is 24, find the other. (product = HCF × LCM)

questions.q: HCF × LCM = 144 and LCM = 36. Find the HCF.

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up two blinking-light times, find their HCF and use HCF × LCM = product to get the LCM. Then explain why 3/8 ends but 1/3 repeats, and why √2 is irrational but √9 isn't.

revisionCard.summary: Real numbers = rational (fractions; decimals end or repeat) + irrational (√2, π; never end or repeat). HCF × LCM = product of the two numbers. A fraction terminates only if its lowest-term denominator has just 2s and 5s. √ of a perfect square is rational.

rememberIt.hook: Every number is rational (a fraction) or irrational (never-ending, non-repeating)
rememberIt.unpack: Rational = p/q. Irrational = sqrt(2), pi, ... A decimal that ends OR repeats is rational.

studentQuestions.a: Yes - 0.333... = 1/3. A decimal that ends OR repeats is rational. Only never-ending, never-repeating decimals (like sqrt 2) are irrational.
You can prove no fraction p/q equals it - assuming one exists leads to a contradiction. So it cannot be written as a fraction.
